subject: Mathematics
chapter: Another Peek Beyond the Point (Solid Shapes)

key_points: Faces (F) are the flat surfaces, Edges (E) are the lines, and Vertices (V) are the corners
A 'Net' is a 2D skeleton that can be folded to form a 3D solid
Oblique sketches give a visual sense of a solid without exact measurements
Isometric sketches use a dot grid to maintain the proportional lengths of edges
examples: Cuboid: 6 Faces, 12 Edges, 8 Vertices [Image showing faces, edges, and vertices of a cuboid]
Net of a cube: Six squares connected in a 'T' or 'cross' shape
Isometric drawing: A cube drawn on dot paper where all edges appear equal
Oblique drawing: A cube drawn on squared paper where the front face is a square
formulas: For a Cube/Cuboid $\rightarrow F=6, V=8, E=12$
Isometric Scale $\rightarrow$ Edges are drawn in the ratio 1:1 to their actual length
exam_tips: When counting edges, be careful not to count the same line twice
In a net, ensure that no two faces will overlap when folded
Use isometric dot paper for technical drawings to keep the scale accurate
A cylinder net consists of two circles and one rectangle
